mission.title: Install and Configure Continuous Optimization on Kubernetes
mission.description: Krkn is a chaos and resiliency testing tool for Kubernetes that helps improve performance under failure conditions. By injecting deliberate failures into your Kubernetes clusters, it allows you to assess and enhance their resilience.

steps.title: Clone the repository
steps.description:
Start by cloning the Krkn repository to your local machine using the following command:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/krkn-chaos/krkn.git
cd krkn
```

steps.title: Deploy Krkn using kubectl
steps.description:
Apply the Kubernetes manifests provided in the repository to deploy Krkn. Ensure you are in the cloned repository directory:
```bash
kubectl apply -f deploy/krkn-deployment.yaml --create-namespace --namespace krkn
```

steps.title: Verify the deployment
steps.description:
Check if the Krkn pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace krkn
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Krkn deployment to ensure it runs efficiently:
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment krkn --namespace krkn --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi
```

steps.title: Access the Krkn service
steps.description:
If you need to access the Krkn service, you can port-forward it to your local machine:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/krkn --namespace krkn 8080:80
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Krkn deployment
uninstall.description:
To uninstall Krkn, first delete the deployment:
```bash
kubectl delete -f deploy/krkn-deployment.yaml --namespace krkn
```

uninstall.title: Clean up the namespace
uninstall.description:
Remove the Krkn namespace to clean up any remaining resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace krkn
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of your Krkn deployment:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace krkn -o yaml > krkn-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the repository
upgrade.description:
Pull the latest changes from the repository to get the latest version:
```bash
git pull origin main
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the deployment
upgrade.description:
Apply the updated manifests to upgrade your Krkn deployment:
```bash
kubectl apply -f deploy/krkn-deployment.yaml --namespace krkn
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Krkn pods after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace krkn
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Krkn pods are not starting, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace krkn
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be wrong.

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Krkn service, ensure the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get service --namespace krkn
``` 
If the service is not running, check the deployment and pod status.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are being OOMKilled, consider adjusting the resource limits:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace krkn
``` 
Check the memory usage and adjust the limits accordingly.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Krkn will result in the Krkn pods running without errors and the service being accessible. You can verify this by checking the pod status and accessing the service through port-forwarding.
codeSnippets: kubectl set resources deployment krkn --namespace krkn --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl installed to interact with it.
